title: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queue Depth for Adverse Automated Eligibility Decisions

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA/CPRA §1798.185 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.185 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.16: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queue Depth for Adverse Automated Eligibility Decisions.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.4.16' running on index=itsm sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item" (number, cat_item, opened_at, state, assignment_group), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item" cat_item="privacy_automated_decision_review" state!="Closed" earliest=-180d
| eval opened=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval age_h=round((now()-opened)/3600,1)
| where age_h>72
| stats avg(age_h) as avg_age_h, max(age_h) as max_age_h, count by assignment_group
| sort - count
description: When automated decisions adversely affect consumers, human review pathways must function. Monitoring queue age for appeals ensures operational readiness beyond policy language—supporting transparency and remedy expectations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CCPA/CPRA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:sc_req_item. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map `cat_item` to your catalog entry; (2) tune SLA hours per CPRA operational policy; (3) executive dashboard for legal; (4) integrate with chat alerts; (5) monthly remediation report. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item`.
visualization: Table (queues), Single value (oldest age_h), Column chart by group.

references.title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) — Full Text

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorized privacy team operations** — The privacy operations team performs routine data subject request processing, deletion verifications, and privacy impact assessments that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These are documented in the approved CCPA workflow procedures and should be excluded by the privacy-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled data retention processing** — Automated data lifecycle management jobs execute scheduled deletions and anonymisation runs as part of the approved data retention policy. These produce expected data processing signals that are pre-approved by the Chief Privacy Officer.

3. **Third-party service provider integrations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate data processing under written agreements (CCPA service provider contracts) generate cross-boundary data flows that may trigger detection rules during normal operations.

4. **Consumer-initiated account management** — Consumers exercising self-service account preferences, privacy settings changes, and opt-out selections through the official web portal create processing events that align with normal business operations under the CCPA framework.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `ccpa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved service accounts, scheduled job identifiers, and known service provider IP ranges. The correlation search applies a left join against this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§1798.185`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.185 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.16: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queue Depth for Adverse Automated Eligibility Decisions." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:sc_req_item`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Map `cat_item` to your catalog entry. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** tune SLA hours per CPRA operational policy. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** executive dashboard for legal. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** integrate with chat alerts. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** monthly remediation report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queue Depth for Adverse Automated Eligibility Decisions," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the CCPA/CPRA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `opened_at` (format: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `opened_at` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval opened_at_epoch = strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(opened_at_epoch)
| stats count by opened_at
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(opened_at, "fmt1"), strptime(opened_at, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `opened_at` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the last 180 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.4.16: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queu]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.4.16,reg=CCPA/CPRA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `age_h`, `assignment_group`, `avg_age_h`, `max_age_h`, `opened`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table age_h, assignment_group, avg_age_h, max_age_h, opened
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `age_h`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(age_h) as p50 perc90(age_h) as p90 perc99(age_h) as p99 max(age_h) as hi
```

The current threshold `72` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.4.16: CCPA/CPRA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`queues`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`oldest age_h`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'CCPA/CPRA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Column Chart By Group (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CCPA/CPRA-specific failure modes:**

• Consumer Request categories: CCPA distinguishes Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Opt-Out, Right to Correct, Right to Limit. Detections on 'consumer request' must specify category — the 45-day clock applies differently to each.

• Verifiable Consumer Requests: the consumer identity must be verified 'to a reasonable degree.' Detections measuring request-to-response latency must start the clock at verification complete, not at request receipt — this is a common source of false SLA failures.

• Sale vs share distinction: CPRA broadens 'sale' to include 'share' for cross-context behavioural advertising. Detections on consent-to-share must use the current law wording; a CCPA-only detection misses the CPRA expansion.

• Do Not Sell / Do Not Share signal (GPC): Global Privacy Control must be honoured. Detections must treat GPC-asserting browsers as opted-out irrespective of UI consent state — the technical signal is authoritative.
grandmaExplanation: We when automated decisions adversely affect consumers, human review pathways must function.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Human Review Queu' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CCPA/CPRA control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CCPA/CPRA control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CCPA/CPRA control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CCPA/CPRA programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
